translations.translation:
He Shao (Xingbo)
賀邵 (興伯)
Lived AD 226–275
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 65 (Wu 20)
byZL181
onMarch 16th, 2025

He Shao, styledXingbo, was fromShanyin[山陰],Kuaiji[會稽].A
TheBook of Wustates:[He] Shaowas the grandson ofHe Qiand the son of[He] Jing.
AfterSun Xiuassumed the throne, he was madeCavalier Regular Palace Attendant, then left to beGrand AdministratorofWu commandery[吳郡太守]. During the reign ofSun Hao, he entered to beLeft Army Organizer, then promoted to beDirector of the Central Depositoryand wasGrand Tutor to the Heir Apparent.
[Sun] Haowas so violent and hubristic that government affairs declined day by day.[He] Shaopetitioned to admonish:
“The reason that sage kings of the past understood the conditions of their vast realm despite being ensconced within multiple palace gates and had a clear understanding of the farthest of the eight cardinal directions despite being idle1on top of a prone mat was due to the contributions of employed talents.Your Majesty, with your utmost virtue and your brilliant demeanor, you have inherited the august enterprise. You ought to take the incentive of following the moral way, wield your sacred authority with solemnity, and show your worthiness and express your virtue, thereby bringing order to various politicalaffairs.•••
The literal metaphor for “being idle” is “clothes hanging down and clasped hands” [垂拱衽席]; describes an emperor who does nothing outside.↩
“Ever since a few years ago, the court ranks have become chaotic and complicated, truths and falsehoods have intermixed, there are empty positions everywhere, and civil and military officers laze in their positions. On the outside, there are no garrisons as strong as the tallest mountains while in the inside, there are no officials who can correct shortcomings. The flattering sycophants flap their wings and soar high into heaven, brandishing the power of the court, and stealing glory and wealth. On the contrast, the loyal and good are rejected and curbed, with sincere ministers being harmed. This is why the integrity of upright scholars is being destroyed. Mediocre officials frivolously flatter, anticipating your intentions and catering to your will, and each of them panders to the current situation. People are making judgments contrary to reason and scholars are vomiting out opinions opposed to morality, causing the pure and clean stream to become muddled and tarnished, and loyal ministers to hold their tongue.Your Majestyoccupies a place higher than the ninth heavenly plane and is secluded deep in a chamber with one hundred layers. The words that come out disseminate like the wind and the commands that are given out are followed like shadows. Yet, you bond close with ministers who curry favor and flatter you. Each day, you hear from them that they agree with your intentions. You mistakenly believe that their sort is truly virtuous, that the realm under heaven is already pacified. My mind is uneased; dare I not inform you?
“I heard that the sovereign of a prospering nation is glad to hear their faults, and that a sovereign of a turbulent wasteland is glad to hear their praises. Those who hear their faults become better and attain fortune while those who hear their praises become worse and achieve disaster. This is why the ancient rulers were being courteous to promote talents and why they humbled themselves to seek their faults. An analogy would be the celestial position being as a rapid chariot,2or the tail of tiger serving as a warning.3But as forYour Majesty, you have made the punishments more harsh and you have prohibited honest speech. You dismiss virtuous officials to repel admonishing officials, and you are confused as to the veracity of words being slander or praise, being engrossed in the words of your favorites. In the past, theSupremeAncestor[ofShang] [殷高宗] thought about being aided and dreamed of receiving worthy talents.4Yet,Your Majestyhas forgotten to seek them and neglects them as if they are castaside.•••
It is hard to control a fast-moving vehicle.↩
Even emperors had to watch out to avoid making harmful decisions.↩
Referring to King Wu Ding [武丁]. He is said to have dreamed about such, and after waking up, he ordered a broad search to find sages to help him rule.↩
“FormerRegular AttendantWang Fanwas loyal and scrupulous in his duties, and he was talented in his responsibility of being an assisting aide. Yet, during a bout of drunkenness, he was slain. Recently, theMinister Herald,Ge Xi, was a long-serving minister to the former emperor. He occasionally had his moments of disobedience, but they were just drunken words. Once, after three rounds, he no longer adhered to the custom of [avoiding] taboo matters, yetYour Majestysuddenly became thunderously furious, saying that he was being dismissive and insolent. You made him drink pure wine, which was poisoned and caused him to lose his life. After this, everyonewithin the Seasmourned in their hearts and court ministers lost their ideas.5Those in office regarded withdrawing [from duty] as a blessing and those serving within [the capital] regarded being sent out to serve as fortunate. This is really not the way to maintain and honor the vast deeds [of your ancestors], nor brighten and expand your moralinfluence.•••
The phrase “a mournful heart loses ideas” [悼心失圖] comes to mind here; one cannot think logically when in grief.↩
“He Dingis a man of low character who rushes [to sycophantically serve]. He is beneath mere servants, does not have the slightest ounce of moral conduct, and has zero ability to produce results that even common lackeys like hawks and dogs can do. And yet,Your Majestyadores his flattery and fawning, entrusting him with power and authority. This has made[He] Dingrely on his favor to act without restraint, building up power and fortune for himself. With his mouth, he dictates national policy, and with his hands, he fiddles with divine authority. This has dimmed your radiance of sun and moon, and obstructed the path of the noble man. When men of low character seek approval, they will certainly propose insidious profits.[He] Dinghas enacted labor, dispatching the guards at theYangzi River[江] to drive away the mi-deer, set up snares at mountains and mounds, mow down trees and weeds, and gather wild animals from all places and herd them into a multi-fenced enclosure. There are no parts thereof that benefit the current [situation] and wastes our resources. The troops are exhausted from doing transports, and men have spent their whole strength on driving away [animals]. The old and young are famished and freezing, and men of high and low rank are resentful.
“I have seen changes in the heavens; ever since recent years,Yin and Yanghave been in disorder, the four seasons have gone against their times, and there are solar eclipses and earthquakes. During the middle of the summer, there was frost falling down. Examining the ancient canons, they all [attribute this to] the energy of yin overpowering yang, which is brought about by villains abusing power. I once read many books and biographies, and I have verified that actions bring about portents, good or ill, hence the chilliness. In the past, theSupremeAncestor[高宗] cultivated himself [in virtue] to get rid of the strange appearance of the tripod and pheasant,6and theRevered[Duke] of Song [宋景公] increased his virtue to repel the change of the Shimmering Planet.7I hopeYour Majestydreads the warning rebuke of August Heaven, pursues the path of repelling disaster as the two lords did, reads about the merits of past dynasties employing the worthy, becomes aware about the faults of giving out erroneous conferments that are happening now. You should make the court ranks incorrupt and organized, and you should praise and recruit outstanding talents, while you should release and repel sycophantic villains, and you should curb malevolent forces. Those sorts of [wicked] people should absolutely no longer be utilized. You should broaden and extend [opportunities] to those in stagnation,8tolerate and accept candid advice, and respectfully worship the will of the Cosmos. Those being so, your grand moral influence will spread widely and the discontent of Heaven and Mankind will beremoved.•••
When King Wu Ding was offering sacrifices, a pheasant flew to the tripod and cawed. The King was so afraid because he thought it was an ill omen, so he worked hard to be a virtuous ruler.↩
Zi Luan [子栾] was the ruler of the State of Song. Once, Mars went into the Heart [心] Mansion, which was the zone of Song. A zone is the astrological belief that certain lands on earth corresponded with certain zones in the heavens. Anything that happened in the zones of heavens, such as comets, supernovas, and other astronomical occurrences, was said to give clues to the future of the lands on earth. In this case, the ministers interpreted Mars being in the Heart Mansion as a herald of disaster. However, Zi Luan rejected attributing it to his chancellor, his people, and the years, saying that he should rely on his chancellor, depend on his people, and that if the years were not good, he would not be king. These were considered virtuous, and there was no disaster.↩
Here, “stagnation” [淹滯] refers to talented people serving in low-ranking positions for a long time, not being used to their fullest potential.↩
“A commentary stated, ‘The country prospers when the people are treated like cherished babes; it declines when the people are treated as mere weeds.’Your Majesty, when you formerly concealed your divine brilliance and hid away your virtues in your eastern realm,9it was with your sagacity, wisdom, and impressive demeanor that you soared high like a dragon to respond to Heaven. Those within theFour Seaswere craning their necks [in anticipation] and those from all eight cardinal directions were wiping their eyes [to watch excitedly], believing the reformation of theAccomplished[King]and thePeaceful[King] [ofZhou] [周康王] would certainly flourish after that morning and evening.10But ever since you ascended the throne, the laws and prohibitions have become more harsh, and the taxes and requisitions have become more grandiose. The eunuchs within your palace are spread across the provinces and commanderies, arbitrarily starting laborious projects and competing with each other to create illegal profits. The common people are suffering from their shuttle and reed being exhausted,11and the commonfolk are exhausted from incessant requests, hence the old and the young are famished and impoverished, with many households having gaunt expressions. The senior officials of those areas are afraid of having faults, so they make their laws strict and their retributions harsh, causing the people to suffer in to their pursuit of obtaining [resources]. This is something people cannot endure. Many a family has been divided, the sorrowful cries disturbing the peaceful air. What is more, the defending garrisons at the border of theYangzi River[江] have to expand our territory and defend the border from trouble. They should be trained especially well so that we can await any emergency. However, the occurrence of taxes and requisitions is as smoke becoming clouds gathering together.12The cotton and coarse material of their clothes are worn out, and there is not enough food to provide for each morning and evening. If they come out, they face the predicament of blades and arrowheads, and if they come back, they face the misery of having nothing to sustain themselves with. This is why fathers and sons are abandoning each other and why the traitors are successful. I hope thatYour Majestywill lessen taxes, remove inconveniences, provide relief to the poor, reduce everything that is not needed urgently, remove prohibitions, and simplify laws. In this way, thelands within the Seaswill be happy to serve and your great moral influence would extend to allplaces.•••
Referring to whenSun Haowas still a prince.↩
These two kings of theZhou dynastypresided over an era of peace and stability despite their young ages.↩
Any material the common people could have used a loom on has been taken for the projects.↩
Here, “smoke becoming clouds gathering together” [煙至雲集] is a metaphor for a large number of occurrences.↩
“The people are the roots of a nation and food is the livelihood of the people. Now our nation has been without one year worth of food reserves and families have no stockpiles that can last for months, yet those within the harem are sitting idly by and consuming number over ten thousand people! In the inside, there are grievances of separation,13and on the outside, there are expenses that are wasteful. This has made the military warehouses and food depositories to be empty of even useless things and the people to lack even alcohol dregs and brans. What is more,our northern enemy, with focused eyes, is watching the decline of our empire. Yet,Your Majestyis relying only on your own might and virtue, supposing that the enemy is not coming, ignoring the poverty within theFour Seas, and underestimating our enemies as being unable to cause trouble. This is truly not a lasting strategy nor a plan devised forvictory.•••
The women are complaining by being taken away from their homes to do nothing in the harem.↩
“In the past, theGreatAugust Emperorstrived hard and worked bitterly to establish a foundation in the south part of the central realm, occupy rivers and mountains, and expand his land to tens of thousands ofli. Even though he received the aid of Heaven, it was really due to the efforts of others. The surplus fortunes and remaining blessings have been passed down ontoYour Majesty.Your Majestyshould strive to maximize your virtue and talent so as to honor the achievements of the past. Love the people, train the soldiers, and maintain the past rules. How can you forget the hard work of yourprestigious ancestorand treat a great legacy that was hard to obtain so lightly? Have you forgotten about realms being beyond saving and have you ignored the major changes of declines and rises [of empires]? I heard that declines and rises are never constant, that good fortune and ill fortune come from men. We cannot rely on the boundary of theYangzi River[江] forever, and if we are unable to defend it, even a bundle of reeds can sail through.
“In the past, theQinestablished the title ofAugust Emperorand occupied the natural defenses of Mount Xiao [崤山] andHangu Pass[函谷關], but they did not cultivate their virtue and moral influence, and they made their laws and punishments harsh and cruel, thereby poisoning living people and preventing loyal ministers from speaking. More recently, theLiu clanoccupied the strategic Three Passes and had defenses as firm as overlapping mountains; they could be said to have been a metal fortress and a stone house—impregnable—and had a legacy stretching for tens of thousands of generations. However, by giving appointments to the unworthy, they collapsed in a single morning. Thelordand his ministers tied ropes around their necks,14becoming mere servants together. Such is a clear lesson for our times and a manifest warning before oureyes.•••
In ancient times, a person indicating their surrender would tie a rope around their necks.↩
“MayYour Majestyexamine past matters, observe current changes in the world, expand our foundation, strengthen our roots, and forgo your personal feelings to follow the moral way. In that way, the governance of theAccomplished[King]and thePeaceful[King] [ofZhou] [周康王] will prosper and the blessings of yoursacred grandfatherwill flourish!”
This petition was memorialized.[Sun] Haodeeply resented him for it.
When[He] Shaoperformed his duties, he was pure and upright, and so he was feared by those intimate and close [withSun Hao]. They all slandered[He] ShaoandLou Xuan, alleging that they were ruining national affairs. The two were heavily rebuked;[Lou] Xuanwas exiled to the southern provinces whileHe Shaowas absolved and returned to his office. Later,[He] Shaosuffered a stroke and could not utter any words. He left his office for several months, but[Sun] Haosuspected that he was ill as an excuse, arrested him, and sent him to a wine cellar, where he was flogged and tortured about one thousand times.[He] Shaonever spoke a single word and he was ultimately killed. His family was exiled toLinhai[臨海]. In addition, an edict was issued that executed the descendants of[Lou] Xuan. That year was the first year of theera [275].[He] Shaowas forty-nine at thetime.B
The son of[He] Shaowas [He] Xun [賀循], styled Yanxian [彥先].
TheBook of Jinby Yu Yu states: When [He] Xun was met with familial disaster, he was exiled to the ocean coasts. AfterWuwas pacified, he returned to his hometown. In conduct, he was noble and stern, and fellow children could not match up to him. In words, conduct, actions, and movement, he would always be polite and modest. He was fond of learning and had extensive knowledge, especially being apt in the Three Ritual Classics.15He was nominated asOutstanding Talentand becamePrefectofYangxian[陽羨令] andPrefectofWukang[武康令].Gu Rong,Lu Ji, andLu Yunpetitioned to recommend [He] Xun,stating:•••
Here, the Three Ritual Classics refers to theEtiquette and Ceremonies[儀禮], theRites of Zhou[周禮], and theBook of Rites[禮記]↩
“We have witnessed the deep and outstanding virtue and capability that thePrefectofWuxing[吳興令] andPrefectofWukang[武康令], He Xun, has. He is talented at discerning those of incorrupt hearts from afar, and he holds pure morality dear to him. In style and conduct, he is solemn and stern, and in the three counties that he governed, his laws and governances were severe and serious. He was devoted to knowing his subordinate counties such that he recorded their names into a collection. He has come from thenew state, so the court has no knowledge of him. He is scrupulous even when assigned to remote areas, not seeking to better himself. The years go by fast, but there are no stairs to officialdom. This is really what makes the foolish and wise of provinces and localities despondent. We servants of yours are ordinary talents who have long been given flamboyant promotions and received your vast favor. We are very ashamed to enjoy being humble court officials.16If we knew of good gentlemen, but stuck to our jobs and said nothing, we fear that we would have done the misdeed of obscuring talents. Hence, we are unable to bear our foolishness and shallowness any further: we solemnly brave death to make this petitionknown!”•••
The literal term for “humble court officials” is “last at court” [朝末], which means they were placed at the far end of the court to show their low status. It is not literal in this case, as it is often used in literary works to refer to oneself in humble terms.↩
Long after, he was summoned to serve asRetainer of the Heir Apparent. When Shi Bing [石冰] overranYang province[揚州], [He] Xun also gathered an army, and when the incident was pacified, he shut his gates and did not come out. When Chen Min [陳敏] rebelled, he made [He] Xun theInterior CensorofDanyang[丹陽內史], but [He] Xun claimed illness and firmly declined. [Chen] Min did not dare force him. At the time, among the prominent magnates in theJiangdong region[江東], there were none of them that did not accept peerages and positions from [Chen] Min, and only [He] Xun and Zhu Dan [朱誕] of the same commandery were not ensnared by the treacherous net. He was later madeInterior CensorofWu state[吳國內史], but he did not assume it. When theInauguralAugust Emperorserved asGeneral who Guards the East, he invited [He] Xun to serve as ArmyMajor, and when the theEmperorbecamePrinceofJin[晉王], he appointed [He] Xun to serveDirector of the Central Depository, who firmly declined and did not accept it. He was transferred to becomeMinister of CeremoniesandGrand Tutorfor theHeir Apparent. At the time, the imperial court was recently established, and there were many disputes and arguments what actions to take. The regulations and procedures of the ancestral temples were all established by [He] Xun, and he would consulted by the imperial court, being a master of Ruism at that time. At the age of sixty, he died in the second year of theera [319]. He was posthumously madeExcellency of Worksand given the title of theSolemn[穆]. The writings and opinions that [He] Xun authored have all been passed down to the world. His son, [He] Xi [賀隰], served asGrand AdministratorofLinhai[臨海太守].
ㅤ
